title: New Analytical Model for Constant-Head Pumping: Considering Rate-Dependent Factor at Well Screen

research.question: How does rate-dependent non-Darcy head loss at a fully penetrating well screen affect constant-head pumping drawdown, flowrate, and estimated aquifer parameters?
research.system: A fully penetrating constant-head well in a homogeneous confined aquifer with wellbore skin, nonlinear screen loss, and a time-dependent finite outer boundary.
research.inputData: Maintained well drawdown, well radius, transmissivity, storativity, skin factor, rate-dependent factor, time, and cumulative-volume records from two constant-head injection tests.
research.method: Represent skin and rate-dependent losses at the inner boundary, approximate transient propagation with a steady radial equation and moving outer radius, derive drawdown and wellbore flowrate, verify numerically, and fit field data by nonlinear least squares.
research.benchmark: The analytical drawdown is compared with an implicit finite-difference solution, and field fits are compared with Jacob-Lohman and Hurst-type solutions using SEE and ME.
research.uncertainty: Normalized sensitivities show parameter influence, while the moving-boundary approximation, fully penetrating confined geometry, and two available injection tests constrain generalization.
research.conclusion: Including both skin and rate-dependent well-screen loss improves the CHIT1 fit and changes inferred well condition; the benefit is smaller for CHIT2, where the compared models estimate similar transmissivity.

narrative.throughline: Hold the well head fixed and follow the declining flowrate through a damaged, non-Darcy screen before the pressure disturbance reaches an expanding outer radius.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: A constant-head test infers aquifer properties from the flow needed to maintain a prescribed well drawdown.
narrative.stakes: If screen loss grows with flowrate, assigning all early resistance to aquifer transmissivity or constant skin biases parameter interpretation.
narrative.resolution: A rate-dependent inner boundary and time-dependent radius yield closed drawdown and flowrate expressions that can be checked numerically and fitted to injection-test records.

background.premise: Constant-head pumping tests are used to estimate aquifer properties, particularly in low-permeability settings.
background.visibleEvidence: A controller holds well drawdown fixed while a flow meter records the changing injection or pumping rate.
background.whyNow: Parameter inference depends on correctly assigning observed head loss between aquifer and well.
background.boundary: The study treats a fully penetrating well in a confined aquifer.
background.sourceExcerpt: The test for constant-head pumping (CHP) is commonly performed
background.motionPurpose: Fix drawdown while allowing the wellbore flowrate to evolve.
background.stepDetail: Lock the well-water level and animate a declining rate gauge.
background.transitionBridge: The measured rate includes resistance concentrated at the well screen.

background.premise: Non-Darcy head loss at the well screen increases with pumping rate rather than behaving as constant skin loss.
background.visibleEvidence: Near-screen loss bends upward as the flowrate gauge increases, unlike a straight constant-skin offset.
background.whyNow: Early high rates can make nonlinear screen loss large enough to alter fitted aquifer parameters.
background.boundary: The paper parameterizes the effect at the screen; it does not resolve pore-scale turbulence.
background.sourceExcerpt: the studies in petroleum engineering indicate that the head loss due to the non-Darcy ﬂow is proportional to the pumping rate
background.motionPurpose: Distinguish nonlinear screen loss from the constant skin component.
background.stepDetail: Split total well loss into a fixed-slope skin term and a rate-dependent curved term.
background.transitionBridge: Both losses enter the well-aquifer inner boundary.

methods.input: Equation 30 with k equal to 2 and fitted CHIT well-loss coefficients
methods.output: C values of 1163.51 min2/m5 for well 2 and 0.97 min2/m5 for well 5
methods.validity: The calculation evaluates the Rorabaugh-type Equation 30 with k equal to 2 using the fitted results for the two reported constant-head injection tests.

methods.motionPurpose: Calculate the source-reported well-loss quantity before interpreting well condition.
methods.stepDetail: Insert each test's fitted terms into Equation 30 with k equal to 2 and reveal its reported C value.
methods.transitionBridge: The calculated C values enter the source's well-condition threshold classification.

methods.input: Calculated Equation 30 C values and the Kurtulus deteriorating-condition threshold
methods.validity: The classification compares the calculated C values with the cited threshold C greater than 0.5 min2/m5 for k between 1 and 2; it is a well-performance criterion rather than an aquifer-property classification.
methods.locator: PDF page 6, Section 3.5 Performance of test wells, Equation (30)
methods.sourceExcerpt: values indicate that both test wells were not properly designed
methods.visualObject: Two calculated C values positioned above the well-condition threshold
methods.motionPurpose: Separate the source's test-well classification from the aquifer fit ranking.
methods.stepDetail: Place each calculated C value on the criterion scale and mark both test wells with the reported classification.
methods.transitionBridge: The owned classification returns to the two physical injection wells.

return.visualObject: Wells 2 and 5 with their Equation 30 C values positioned against the deteriorating-condition threshold
return.claim: Applying the source well-loss criterion to the fitted coefficients classifies both test wells as not properly designed or developed.
return.claimBoundary: The return is limited to the Equation 30 Rorabaugh-type criterion with k equal to 2 and the cited Kurtulus threshold; it classifies test-well performance and does not by itself identify the construction defect or reclassify aquifer properties.

visual.primaryObject: Fully penetrating constant-head well in a confined aquifer
visual.accentObject: Skin and rate-dependent head-loss elements at the well screen
visual.measuredQuantity: Aquifer drawdown, wellbore flowrate, and cumulative injected volume
visual.geometryNotes: Use concentric radial aquifer rings, a fixed well-head marker, and an expanding zero-drawdown outer radius; separate the near-screen loss into linear skin and nonlinear rate-dependent components before fitting cumulative-volume traces.
